project.name: Atlas Workbench
project.purpose: A modern Linux desktop control panel that wraps the user's existing llama.cpp binary, exposing every server flag, model operation, and fine-tuning parameter through clean widgets instead of command-line memorization.

thesis: Atlas Workbench is a professional Linux desktop control panel that gives you every llama.cpp capability — server, models, settings, fine-tuning, monitoring — through clean modern widgets, with zero command-line memorization.
excellent_outcome: I open Atlas Workbench, browse to my GGUF model, click Start, watch the server come alive in the log panel, tweak every flag through dropdowns and sliders, fine-tune a model with form controls, monitor my hardware in real time, and when something breaks I get a clear message telling me exactly what to fix — all without ever touching a terminal.
unacceptable_outcomes: Any llama.cpp flag that is not exposed as a widget, forcing the user back to the terminal
Fine-tuning runs but produces no usable model or fails without a clear error message
The UI looks dated, unprofessional, or uses legacy widget aesthetics
The server process crashes or hangs and the user receives no feedback
Settings do not persist between sessions
non_negotiable_promises: Every llama.cpp server flag is controllable through a GUI widget — no terminal needed for any flag
Fine-tuning must complete end-to-end and produce a trained model file at the specified output path
Errors are always shown as clear in-app messages in plain language, never silently swallowed
Settings persist between sessions via a config file
The app is distributed as a .deb or AppImage and appears in the Linux app menu

user_workflows.name: Start server with a loaded model
user_workflows.trigger: User selects a GGUF model file, configures server settings, and clicks Start
user_workflows.happy_path: The app launches the llama.cpp server binary as a child process with the selected model and all configured flags, the server starts successfully, the log panel shows stdout/stderr output, and the System Monitor tab begins showing real-time metrics
user_workflows.proof: Scenario test: select model, set port and host, click Start, assert server process is running, assert log panel shows server startup output, assert server responds on the configured port

user_workflows.name: Fine-tune a model
user_workflows.trigger: User navigates to the Fine-tuning tab, selects a dataset, sets learning rate and epochs, specifies an output path, and clicks Start Training
user_workflows.happy_path: The app launches the llama.cpp finetune binary with all specified parameters, training progresses, and a trained model file is produced at the output path
user_workflows.proof: Scenario test: configure finetune parameters, start training, assert process runs, assert output model file exists at the specified path after completion

user_workflows.name: Recover from an error
user_workflows.trigger: User points to a nonexistent model file or the server crashes mid-run
user_workflows.happy_path: The app shows a clear in-app error message in plain language explaining what went wrong, the user fixes the issue, and retries successfully
user_workflows.proof: Scenario test: point to a nonexistent GGUF file, click Start, assert a visible error message appears, fix the path, click Start again, assert server starts

assumptions.assumption: The user has a working llama.cpp build with both the server and finetune binaries available on their Linux machine
assumptions.risk: If the binary is missing or broken, the app cannot function and must detect and report this clearly
assumptions.validation: On first launch, the app checks that the configured binary path exists and is executable; if not, it prompts the user to locate it with a clear message

assumptions.assumption: The llama.cpp server and finetune binaries accept flags via command-line arguments that can be mapped to widgets
assumptions.risk: If llama.cpp changes its flag interface, widgets may pass invalid arguments
assumptions.validation: Integration test: launch the server with a representative set of flags and verify it starts without error; document the llama.cpp version the app is tested against

assumptions.assumption: GPU metrics are readable on Linux via sysinfo or a similar mechanism
assumptions.risk: If GPU monitoring is not available, the System Monitor tab may show incomplete data
assumptions.validation: Test on a machine with and without a GPU; if GPU metrics are unavailable, show a clear 'GPU not detected' message instead of a blank panel

assumptions.assumption: GGUF model files are stored in a user-specified directory that the app can browse
assumptions.risk: If the directory is invalid or inaccessible, model browsing fails silently
assumptions.validation: Test with a valid directory, an empty directory, and a nonexistent path; each must produce appropriate UI feedback

handwave_bans: Agents must not create a widget that does not wire to a real llama.cpp flag — every dropdown, slider, and input must map to an actual command-line argument passed to the binary
Agents must not stub the fine-tuning workflow — it must launch the real finetune binary and produce a real output model file
Agents must not use a legacy or default-looking widget toolkit — the UI must use modern React/Tailwind components with professional styling
Agents must not silently swallow errors — every process failure, missing file, or invalid configuration must surface as a visible in-app message
acceptance_laws: Every visible control must be wired to real behavior — clicking it must change a llama.cpp argument, launch a process, or update persisted settings
Fine-tuning is only accepted as done when a trained model file exists at the specified output path after the process completes
The app is only accepted as installable when it can be packaged as a .deb or AppImage and launched from the Linux app menu
Settings are only accepted as persistent when closing and reopening the app restores all previously saved configuration values
evidence_laws: Every feature must be proven by a scenario test with pass/fail evidence — no feature is done based on visual inspection alone
Server launch must be proven by asserting the child process is running and the configured port responds
Fine-tuning must be proven by asserting the output model file exists after training completes
Error handling must be proven by triggering a known failure condition and asserting a visible error message appears in the UI

intent_ledger.source: I want a really nice GUI for using llama.cpp so I dont have to memorize any commands or flags
intent_ledger.promise: Every llama.cpp flag is exposed as a widget — zero command-line memorization required
intent_ledger.acceptance: Every llama.cpp server flag has a corresponding widget in the Settings tab, and changing a widget changes the flag passed to the binary

intent_ledger.source: I want it to have a very modern look and feel. Not some sort of dated windows XP sort of look. Professional and modern is what I want.
intent_ledger.promise: The UI must look professional and modern, using React/Tailwind components — no legacy widget aesthetics
intent_ledger.acceptance: The app uses modern React components with Tailwind CSS styling; visual review confirms no dated or legacy widget appearance

intent_ledger.source: Nice looking widgets and dropdown menus. Nice and organized tab layout
intent_ledger.promise: The app has an organized tab layout with nice-looking widgets and dropdown menus
intent_ledger.acceptance: The main interface has clearly organized tabs (Server, Models, Settings, Fine-tuning, System Monitor) with modern widgets in each

intent_ledger.source: Its for running and monitoring my local llm server. I want to easily load/unload models, fine tune, tweak settings, maximize performance of my hardware
intent_ledger.promise: Full control panel: server start/stop, model load/unload, settings tweak, fine-tuning, and hardware performance monitoring
intent_ledger.acceptance: All five tabs are functional and each maps to real llama.cpp functionality

intent_ledger.source: including a tab to monitor system resources so you know exactly whats going on with your system
intent_ledger.promise: A System Monitor tab showing real-time CPU, RAM, and GPU metrics
intent_ledger.acceptance: System Monitor tab displays real-time CPU, RAM, and GPU metrics updating at least once per second

intent_ledger.source: Already built — I have llama.cpp ready — Keeps the first version focused on the GUI; the app just points to your existing binary.
intent_ledger.promise: The app points to the user's existing llama.cpp binary — it does not download or build it
intent_ledger.acceptance: First launch prompts for the binary path; the app never downloads or builds llama.cpp

intent_ledger.source: Everything including fine-tuning — More ambitious first build; fine-tuning adds significant complexity but you get the full vision at once.
intent_ledger.promise: Fine-tuning is included in the first version and must work end-to-end
intent_ledger.acceptance: Fine-tuning tab launches the finetune binary, training completes, and the output model file exists at the specified path

intent_ledger.source: A downloadable package like a .deb or AppImage — Easy to install with a single file, shows up in your app menu like any normal application.
intent_ledger.promise: The app is distributed as a .deb or AppImage and appears in the Linux app menu
intent_ledger.acceptance: The app can be packaged as a .deb or AppImage and launched from the Linux application menu

intent_ledger.source: I can do all the above AND fine-tune a model successfully — Requires the full pipeline including training to work, which is a higher bar.
intent_ledger.promise: The full pipeline including fine-tuning must actually work — not just look like it works
intent_ledger.acceptance: Scenario test proves fine-tuning produces a real output model file

intent_ledger.source: Show a clear error message and let you fix it and try again — Keeps you in control; the app explains what went wrong in plain language so you can adjust and retry.
intent_ledger.promise: Errors are shown as clear in-app messages in plain language, and the user can fix and retry
intent_ledger.acceptance: Every failure condition produces a visible in-app error message with a plain-language explanation and a retry option

intent_ledger.source: Scenario tests for the critical workflows - prove the most important user paths with pass/fail evidence.
intent_ledger.promise: Critical workflows are proven by scenario tests with pass/fail evidence
intent_ledger.acceptance: Each feature has scenario tests defined and passing that prove the critical user paths

intent_ledger.source: Preserve current user workflows and data - new work should not break existing launch, save, or run paths.
intent_ledger.promise: Existing user workflows and data must not be broken by new work
intent_ledger.acceptance: Config file format changes are backward-compatible; existing settings are preserved across updates

intent_ledger.source: Finish missing real behavior - replace placeholders and incomplete flows with working code.
intent_ledger.promise: No placeholder buttons or incomplete flows — every control maps to real behavior
intent_ledger.acceptance: Every visible control is wired to real llama.cpp functionality — no stubs or placeholders

intent_ledger.source: Tool permissions, memory, and confirmation rules - keeps autonomous behavior predictable and safe.
intent_ledger.promise: Agent operates with locked tool permissions, memory rules, and confirmation rules
intent_ledger.acceptance: Agent rules are defined in conventions: no direct file system access from frontend, no modifying llama.cpp binary, confirm before changing config format
